content.text:
**Functions.** A function $f$ assigns to each element in its domain 
exactly one element in its range. We write $f(x)$ for the output 
corresponding to input $x$. Functions can be represented verbally, 
algebraically, numerically (tables), or graphically.

**Domain and Range.** The domain is the set of all valid inputs; the 
range is the set of all actual outputs. Common domain restrictions arise 
from division by zero and even roots of negative numbers.

**Graphs.** The graph of $f$ consists of all points $(x, f(x))$. A 
curve represents a function if and only if it passes the vertical line 
test. Key features include intercepts, intervals of increase/decrease, 
and symmetry.
**Transformations.** Starting from a basic function, we can:

items: Reflect across axes by negating $f(x)$ or $x$

content.text:
**Combining Functions.** Functions can be added, subtracted, 
multiplied, divided, and composed. For composition, $(f \circ g)(x) = 
f(g(x))$.

**Inverse Functions.** A one-to-one function has an inverse that 
"undoes" it: $f^{-1}(f(x)) = x$ and $f(f^{-1}(x)) = x$. The graph of 
$f^{-1}$ is the reflection of the graph of $f$ across $y = x$.
